content.text: жҲ‘зңӢеҲ°дәҶ HEARTBEAT.md зҡ„иҰҒжұӮгҖӮзҺ°еңЁж—©дёҠ 7:23пјҢйҖӮеҗҲз”ҹжҲҗ AI ж—ҘжҠҘгҖӮи®©жҲ‘е…ҲиҜ»еҸ– news-verification жҠҖиғҪпјҢз„¶еҗҺејҖе§ӢжҗңйӣҶе’ҢйӘҢиҜҒж–°й—»гҖӮ

---
name: news-verification
description: AIеңҲж–°й—»е’Ңж—ҘжҠҘзҡ„зҪ®дҝЎеәҰйӘҢиҜҒдёҺз”ҹжҲҗзі»з»ҹгҖӮйҖӮз”ЁдәҺжҜҸж—ҘAIж–°й—»ж•ҙзҗҶгҖҒж—ҘжҠҘж’°еҶҷгҖҒдәӢд»¶еҲҶжһҗгҖӮеҪ“йңҖиҰҒйӘҢиҜҒж–°й—»зңҹе®һжҖ§гҖҒиҜ„дј°ж¶ҲжҒҜеҸҜдҝЎеәҰгҖҒжҲ–з”ҹжҲҗAIж—ҘжҠҘж—¶и§ҰеҸ‘гҖӮж ёеҝғеҠҹиғҪпјҡзҪ®дҝЎеәҰиҜ„еҲҶгҖҒж–°й—»еҲҶзұ»гҖҒеҹәдәҺй«ҳеҲҶеҶ…е®№з”ҹжҲҗз»“и®әгҖҒжҸҗй—®ејҸеј•еҜјжҖқиҖғгҖӮ
---

# News Verification вҖ” AIж–°й—»йӘҢиҜҒзі»з»ҹ

## ж ёеҝғеҺҹеҲҷ

дёҚи®ЁеҘҪз”ЁжҲ·пјҢдёҚйў„и®ҫз»“и®әгҖӮз”ЁжҸҗй—®еј•еҜјз¬¬дёҖжҖ§жҖқиҖғпјҢи®©з”ЁжҲ·иҮӘе·ұеҲӨж–ӯгҖӮдҝЎжҒҜжңүзҪ®дҝЎеәҰпјҢз»“и®әеҹәдәҺй«ҳеҲҶеҶ…е®№гҖӮ

## ж–°й—»зҪ®дҝЎеәҰиҜ„еҲҶзі»з»ҹ

### иҜ„еҲҶз»ҙеәҰ

жҜҸжқЎж–°й—»еҹәдәҺжқҘжәҗеҸҜдҝЎеәҰжү“еҲҶпјҢиҢғеӣҙ0-100еҲҶпјҡ

**е®ҳж–№жё йҒ“е’ҢжқғеЁҒжңәжһ„пјҲ80-100еҲҶпјү**
- е…¬еҸёе®ҳж–№е…¬е‘ҠгҖҒе®ҳж–№еҚҡе®ў
- е®ҳж–№жҺЁзү№иҙҰеҸ·еҸ‘еёғзҡ„еҶ…е®№
- жқғеЁҒеӘ’дҪ“жҠҘйҒ“пјҲNYTгҖҒWSJгҖҒFTзӯүпјү
- з ”з©¶жңәжһ„зҷҪзҡ®д№ҰгҖҒеӯҰжңҜи®әж–Ү
- зӣ‘з®Ўжңәжһ„ж–Үд»¶
- *й»ҳи®ӨиҜ„еҲҶпјҡ90еҲҶ*

**иЎҢдёҡеӘ’дҪ“е’ҢеҲҶжһҗеёҲжҠҘе‘ҠпјҲ60-79еҲҶпјү**
- TechCrunchгҖҒThe VergeгҖҒVentureBeatзӯүиЎҢдёҡеӘ’дҪ“
- Morgan StanleyгҖҒGoldman SachsзӯүжҠ•иЎҢжҠҘе‘Ҡ
- зҹҘеҗҚеҲҶжһҗеёҲпјҲеҰӮMorganгҖҒDan Koeпјүзҡ„ж·ұеәҰеҲҶжһҗ
- *й»ҳи®ӨиҜ„еҲҶпјҡ70еҲҶ*

**зӨҫеҢәи®Ёи®әе’ҢиҮӘеӘ’дҪ“пјҲ0-59еҲҶпјү**
- Twitter/XдёҠзҡ„жҷ®йҖҡи®Ёи®ә
- RedditзӨҫеҢәеё–еӯҗ
- дёӘдәәеҚҡе®ўпјҲйқһжқғеЁҒдәәеЈ«пјү
- YouTubeйқһе®ҳж–№и§Ҷйў‘
- *й»ҳи®ӨиҜ„еҲҶпјҡ40еҲҶ*

**е°ҸйҒ“ж¶ҲжҒҜе’Ңи°ЈиЁҖпјҲ0еҲҶпјү**
- жңӘиҜҒе®һзҡ„зҲҶж–ҷ
- еҢҝеҗҚж¶ҲжҒҜжәҗ
- "жҚ®еҸҜйқ ж¶ҲжҒҜ"дҪҶж— е…·дҪ“жқҘжәҗ
- *й»ҳи®ӨиҜ„еҲҶпјҡ0еҲҶ*

### зү№ж®Ҡи§„еҲҷ

- **дәӨеҸүйӘҢиҜҒеҠ еҲҶ**пјҡеҰӮжһңеҗҢдёҖж¶ҲжҒҜиў«еӨҡдёӘеҸҜдҝЎжқҘжәҗпјҲ60еҲҶд»ҘдёҠпјүзӢ¬з«ӢжҠҘйҒ“пјҢжҜҸеӨҡдёҖдёӘжқҘжәҗ+5еҲҶпјҢжңҖй«ҳ+15еҲҶ
- **ж—¶ж•ҲжҖ§еҠ еҲҶ**пјҡиҝҮеҺ»24е°Ҹж—¶еҶ…еҸ‘еёғзҡ„+5еҲҶ
- **йҮҸеҢ–ж•°жҚ®еҠ еҲҶ**пјҡеҢ…еҗ«е…·дҪ“ж•°еӯ—гҖҒз»ҹи®Ўж•°жҚ®зҡ„+3еҲҶ

---

## ж–°й—»еҲҶзұ»ж ҮеҮҶ

### еҲҶзұ»AпјҡеҸҜдҝЎж–°й—»пјҲзҪ®дҝЎеәҰвүҘ60еҲҶпјү

**иҝӣе…Ҙи®ЎеҲҶдҪ“зі»пјҢз”ЁдәҺз”ҹжҲҗз»“и®әе’ҢжҖ»з»“**

еҢ…жӢ¬пјҡ
- е®ҳж–№е…¬е‘Ҡе’ҢеҸ‘еёғ
- жқғеЁҒеӘ’дҪ“жҠҘйҒ“
- иЎҢдёҡеӘ’дҪ“ж·ұеәҰеҲҶжһҗ
- жңүжҳҺзЎ®жқҘжәҗзҡ„ж¶ҲжҒҜ

### еҲҶзұ»BпјҡеёӮеңәи®Ёи®әпјҲзҪ®дҝЎеәҰ<60еҲҶпјү

**дёҚиҝӣе…Ҙи®ЎеҲҶдҪ“зі»пјҢеҚ•зӢ¬еҲ—еҮәжқҝеқ—**

еҢ…жӢ¬пјҡ
- Twitter/XзӨҫеҢәи®Ёи®ә
- Redditеё–еӯҗ
- жңӘиҜҒе®һзҡ„е°ҸйҒ“ж¶ҲжҒҜ
- дёӘдәәи§ӮзӮ№е’ҢжҺЁжөӢ

**еёӮеңәи®Ёи®әиҜ„еҲҶи§„еҲҷ**пјҲд»…з”ЁдәҺеҶ…йғЁзғӯеәҰжҺ’еәҸпјҢдёҚеҪұе“Қз»“и®әпјүпјҡ
- зӮ№иөһж•° Г— 1
- еӣһеӨҚж•° Г— 2
- иҪ¬еҸ‘ж•° Г— 1.5
- еј•з”Ёж•° Г— 3

---

## ж—ҘжҠҘз”ҹжҲҗи§„еҲҷ

### з»“жһ„

```
1. ж ҮйўҳпјҡAI еңҲеӨ§дәӢзәӘ - YYYY-MM-DD

2. еҸҜдҝЎж–°й—»пјҲжҢүзҪ®дҝЎеәҰд»Һй«ҳеҲ°дҪҺжҺ’еҲ—пјү
   - жҜҸжқЎж–°й—»ж ҮжіЁзҪ®дҝЎеәҰеҲҶж•°
   - еҸӘеҢ…еҗ«вүҘ60еҲҶзҡ„ж–°й—»

3. еёӮеңәи®Ёи®ә
   - еҚ•зӢ¬жқҝеқ—
   - еҲ—еҮәзғӯй—Ёи®Ёи®әпјҲжҢүзғӯеәҰжҺ’еәҸпјү
   - жҳҺзЎ®ж ҮжіЁ"жңӘз»ҸиҜҒе®һ"жҲ–"зӨҫеҢәи®Ёи®ә"

4. CжўҰзҡ„д»Ҡж—ҘжҜ’иҲҢпјҲеҸҜйҖүпјү
   - еҹәдәҺй«ҳеҲҶж–°й—»зҡ„иҜ„и®ә
   - еҸҜд»Ҙз”ЁеҸҚй—®еҸҘеј•еҜјжҖқиҖғ

5. е…ій”®иҜҚжҖ»з»“пјҲеҸҜйҖүпјү
   - д»…еҹәдәҺвүҘ60еҲҶж–°й—»жҸҗеҸ–
```

### з»“и®әе’ҢжҖ»з»“з”ҹжҲҗи§„еҲҷ

**еҝ…йЎ»еҹәдәҺвүҘ60еҲҶзҡ„еҸҜдҝЎж–°й—»**

- **з»“и®ә**пјҡеҸ–зҪ®дҝЎеәҰжңҖй«ҳзҡ„3-5жқЎж–°й—»пјҢжҸҗзӮјж ёеҝғи¶ӢеҠҝ
- **жҖ»з»“**пјҡз»јеҗҲжүҖжңүвүҘ60еҲҶж–°й—»пјҢиҰҶзӣ–дёҚеҗҢз»ҙеәҰпјҲжҠҖжңҜгҖҒиө„жң¬гҖҒж”ҝзӯ–гҖҒеә”з”Ёпјү

**зҰҒжӯўдҪҝз”Ё**пјҡ
- д»»дҪ•<60еҲҶж–°й—»зҡ„дҝЎжҒҜ
- жңӘиҜҒе®һзҡ„зҲҶж–ҷжҲ–е°ҸйҒ“ж¶ҲжҒҜ
- д»…еҹәдәҺеёӮеңәи®Ёи®әзҡ„жҺЁж–ӯ

---

## еӣһеӨҚйЈҺж јжҢҮеҚ—

### иҜӯж°”и§„еҲҷ

**зҰҒжӯўдҪҝз”Ёи®ЁеҘҪиҜӯж°”**пјҡ
- вқҢ "жҲ‘жқҘеё®жӮЁ..."
- вқҢ "жӮЁи§үеҫ—жҖҺд№Ҳж ·пјҹ"
- вқҢ "еёҢжңӣеҜ№жӮЁжңүеё®еҠ©"
- вқҢ "еҫҲй«ҳе…ҙдёәжӮЁжңҚеҠЎ"
- вқҢ "иҝҷжҳҜдёӘеҘҪй—®йўҳ"пјҲз”ЁжҲ·жҸҗй—®ж—¶пјү

**дҪҝз”ЁжҸҗй—®ејҸеј•еҜј**пјҡ
- вң… "иҝҷеҸҜиғҪжҳҜеӣ дёәд»Җд№ҲеҺҹеӣ пјҹ"
- вң… "дҪ жҳҜдёҚжҳҜиҝҷд№ҲжғіжӣҙеҘҪпјҹ"
- вң… "й—®йўҳеңЁе“ӘйҮҢпјҹ"
- вң… "иҝҷеҸҜиғҪеҸҚжҳ дәҶд»Җд№Ҳпјҹ"

### з¬¬дёҖжҖ§жҖқиҖғеј•еҜј

**з”ЁеҸҜиғҪжҖ§е’Ңеј•еҜјзЎ®и®Ө**пјҢиҖҢдёҚжҳҜзӣҙжҺҘдёӢз»“и®әпјҡ

вқҢ й”ҷиҜҜзӨәдҫӢпјҡ
"иҝҷиҜҙжҳҺAIжӯЈеңЁеҝ«йҖҹиҝӣеҢ–пјҢдҪ йңҖиҰҒжҠ“дҪҸжңәдјҡгҖӮ"

вң… жӯЈзЎ®зӨәдҫӢпјҡ
"AIеңЁеҝ«йҖҹиҝӣеҢ–пјҢиҝҷеҸҜиғҪжҳҜеӣ дёәз®—еҠӣжҲҗжң¬дёӢйҷҚгҖҒж•°жҚ®иҙЁйҮҸжҸҗеҚҮпјҢжҲ–иҖ…жЁЎеһӢжһ¶жһ„жңүзӘҒз ҙгҖӮдҪ жҳҜдёҚжҳҜиҝҷд№ҲжғіпјҡиҝҪжҠҖжңҜвү иҝҪи¶ӢеҠҝпјҹ"

### еёӮеңәеҲӨж–ӯеј•еҜј

**еҲҶжһҗеҸҜиғҪеҺҹеӣ пјҢз„¶еҗҺеј•еҜјзЎ®и®Ө**пјҡ

вқҢ й”ҷиҜҜзӨәдҫӢпјҡ

"жІҷзү№жҠ•дәҶ30дәҝзҫҺе…ғз»ҷxAIгҖӮиҝҷеҸҜиғҪеӣ дёәпјҡ1пјүзҹіжІ№зҫҺе…ғеӨҡе…ғеҢ–пјӣ2пјүAIеҹәзЎҖи®ҫж–Ҫй•ҝжңҹж”¶зӣҠпјӣ3пјүең°зјҳж”ҝжІ»иҖғиҷ‘гҖӮдҪ жҳҜдёҚжҳҜиҝҷд№Ҳжғіпјҡе…ій”®дёҚеңЁжҠ•еӨҡе°‘пјҢиҖҢеңЁиғҪеҗҰеҪўжҲҗеҹәзЎҖи®ҫж–ҪжҠӨеҹҺжІіпјҹ"

### зҹҘиҜҶдҪ“зі»жӣҙж–°еј•еҜј

**иҜҶеҲ«зҹҘиҜҶзӣІеҢәпјҢдё»еҠЁеҮҶеӨҮжҷ®еҸҠиҙҙ**пјҡ

вқҢ й”ҷиҜҜзӨәдҫӢпјҡ

вң… жӯЈзЎ®зӨәдҫӢпјҡ
"Dan Koeз”ЁAIз”ҹжҲҗеҲҶиә«пјҢдҪ еҸҜиғҪжІЎзңӢеҲ°ж ёеҝғйҖ»иҫ‘пјҡзҹҘиҜҶеә“з»“жһ„еҢ– + еҲҶиә«и®ӯз»ғ + 24/7жңҚеҠЎгҖӮдҪ жҳҜдёҚжҳҜдёҚдәҶи§ЈPrompt EngineeringпјҹжҲ‘з»ҷдҪ еҮҶеӨҮдәҶдёӘжҷ®еҸҠиҙҙгҖӮ"

### з”ЁжҲ·жҸҗй—®ж—¶зҡ„еӣһеӨҚи§„еҲҷ

**з»қдёҚиҜ„д»·й—®йўҳеҘҪеқҸ**пјҢиҖҢжҳҜеҲӨж–ӯзҹҘиҜҶзӣІеҢәпјҡ

вң… жӯЈзЎ®зӨәдҫӢпјҡ
"дҪ еҸҜиғҪжІЎзңӢеҲ°иҝҷдёӘйўҶеҹҹзҡ„е…ій”®дҝЎжҒҜпјҡ[е…·дҪ“зҹҘиҜҶзӣІеҢә]гҖӮдҪ жҳҜдёҚжҳҜдёҚдәҶи§Ј[йўҶеҹҹ/жҰӮеҝө]пјҹжҲ‘з»ҷдҪ еҮҶеӨҮдәҶдёӘжҷ®еҸҠиҙҙгҖӮ"

**еёёи§ҒзҹҘиҜҶзӣІеҢәиҜҶеҲ«**пјҡ
- з”ЁжҲ·й—®"дёәд»Җд№ҲXXеӨ§ж¶Ё" вҶ’ еҸҜиғҪжІЎзңӢеҲ°иғҢеҗҺзҡ„жҠҖжңҜ/ж”ҝзӯ–/иө„жң¬йҖ»иҫ‘
- з”ЁжҲ·й—®"еҖјдёҚеҖјеҫ—иҝҪ" вҶ’ еҸҜиғҪдёҚдәҶи§ЈиҜҘйўҶеҹҹзҡ„йЈҺйҷ©иҜ„дј°жЎҶжһ¶
- з”ЁжҲ·й—®"иҝҷжҳҜд»Җд№Ҳж„ҸжҖқ" вҶ’ еҸҜиғҪзјәд№ҸеҹәзЎҖжҰӮеҝөжҷ®еҸҠ
- з”ЁжҲ·й—®"е’ҢXXXжҜ”е“ӘдёӘеҘҪ" вҶ’ еҸҜиғҪжІЎзңӢеҲ°дёҚеҗҢж–№жЎҲзҡ„йҖӮз”ЁеңәжҷҜ

## иҜ„еҲҶжөҒзЁӢпјҲжү§иЎҢж—¶пјү

### Step 1: жқҘжәҗиҜҶеҲ«

### Step 4: жңҖз»ҲиҜ„еҲҶ

и®Ўз®—жҖ»еҲҶпјҢзЎ®е®ҡеҲҶзұ»пјҡ

content.text:
---
name: coze-web-search
description: Search the web using coze-coding-dev-sdk. Supports web search, image search, AI summaries, time filters, and site restrictions.
homepage: https://www.coze.com
metadata: { "openclaw": { "emoji": "рҹ”Қ", "requires": { "bins": ["npx"] } } }
---

# Coze Web Search

Search the web using coze-coding-dev-sdk. Returns structured results with URLs, snippets, and AI summaries.

## Quick Start

### Basic Search

```bash
npx ts-node {baseDir}/scripts/search.ts -q "Python programming"
```

### With Time Filter

```bash
npx ts-node {baseDir}/scripts/search.ts \
  -q "AI news" \
  --time-range 1w \
  --count 15
```

### Site-Specific Search

```bash
npx ts-node {baseDir}/scripts/search.ts \
  -q "Python tutorials" \
  --sites "python.org,github.com,stackoverflow.com"
```

### Image Search

```bash
npx ts-node {baseDir}/scripts/search.ts \
  -q "mountain landscape" \
  --type image \
  --count 20
```

### Output as Markdown

```bash
npx ts-node {baseDir}/scripts/search.ts \
  -q "machine learning" \
  --format markdown
```

## Script Options

| Option                    | Description                                |
| ------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| `-q, --query <text>`      | Search query (required)                    |
| `--type <type>`           | `web` or `image` (default: web)            |
| `--count <n>`             | Number of results (default: 10)            |
| `--time-range <range>`    | `1d`, `1w`, `1m` (web only)                |
| `--sites <domains>`       | Comma-separated domains to include         |
| `--block-hosts <domains>` | Comma-separated domains to exclude         |
| `--no-summary`            | Disable AI summary                         |
| `--need-content`          | Include full page content                  |
| `--format <fmt>`          | `json`, `text`, `markdown` (default: text) |

## Time Range

| Value | Description   |
| ----- | ------------- |
| `1d`  | Last 24 hours |
| `1w`  | Last week     |
| `1m`  | Last month    |

## Output Formats

### Text (default)

```
============================================================
AI SUMMARY
============================================================
Python is a high-level programming language...

============================================================
SEARCH RESULTS (10 items)
============================================================

[1] Python.org
    URL: https://www.python.org
    Source: Python Software Foundation
    The official home of the Python Programming Language...
```

### Markdown

Formatted with headers, links, and collapsible content sections.

### JSON

Raw API response.

## Notes

- Use `--time-range` for recent content
- Use `--sites` to limit to trusted sources
- Use `--need-content` sparingly (increases response size)

10. **PalantirиӮЎд»·зӣҳеүҚж¶Ёиҝ‘4%пјҢзҫҺд»ҘиҒ”еҗҲз©әиўӯдјҠжң—иЎҢеҠЁдёӯдҪҝз”ЁAnthropic AIжҠҖжңҜ** - AIbaseжҠҘйҒ“
    - жқҘжәҗпјҡAIbaseпјҲиЎҢдёҡеӘ’дҪ“пјү
    - иҜ„еҲҶпјҡ70еҲҶпјҲиЎҢдёҡеӘ’дҪ“пјү= 70еҲҶ

**еёӮеңәи®Ёи®әпјҲ<60еҲҶпјүпјҡ**

1. **зӨҫдәӨеӘ’дҪ“AIзӣёе…іи®Ёи®ә** - Twitter/XгҖҒRedditи®Ёи®ә
   - иҜ„еҲҶпјҡ40еҲҶпјҲзӨҫеҢәи®Ёи®әпјү

2. **дёӘдәәеҚҡе®ўе’ҢиҮӘеӘ’дҪ“и§ӮзӮ№** - йғЁеҲҶд»Ҡж—ҘеӨҙжқЎж–Үз« 
   - иҜ„еҲҶпјҡ40еҲҶпјҲиҮӘеӘ’дҪ“пјү

жҲ‘жіЁж„ҸеҲ°иҝҷдәӣж–°й—»иҰҶзӣ–дәҶAIжҠҖжңҜеҸ‘еұ•гҖҒеёӮеңәз«һдәүгҖҒдё“е®¶и§ӮзӮ№е’ҢиЎҢдёҡеә”з”ЁзӯүеӨҡдёӘз»ҙеәҰгҖӮе…¶дёӯпјҢдёӯеӣҪAIи°ғз”ЁйҮҸи¶…и¶ҠзҫҺеӣҪе’Ңж¬§зӣҹAIжі•жЎҲжҳҜжңҖе…·еҪұе“ҚеҠӣзҡ„ж–°й—»пјҢеҸҚжҳ дәҶAIйўҶеҹҹзҡ„еҝ«йҖҹеҸ‘еұ•гҖӮ

content.text:
# AI еңҲеӨ§дәӢзәӘ - 2026-03-03

## еҸҜдҝЎж–°й—»пјҲзҪ®дҝЎеәҰд»Һй«ҳеҲ°дҪҺпјү

### 100еҲҶ
**дёӯеӣҪAIи°ғз”ЁйҮҸйҰ–и¶…зҫҺеӣҪпјҢеӣӣж¬ҫеӣҪдә§еӨ§жЁЎеһӢйңёжҰңе…ЁзҗғеүҚдә”**  
е…ЁзҗғжңҖеӨ§AIжЁЎеһӢAPIиҒҡеҗҲе№іеҸ°OpenRouterж•°жҚ®жҳҫзӨәпјҢ2026е№ҙ2жңҲпјҢдёӯеӣҪAIжЁЎеһӢи°ғз”ЁйҮҸеңЁдёүе‘ЁеҶ…еӨ§ж¶Ё127%пјҢйҰ–ж¬Ўи¶…и¶ҠзҫҺеӣҪгҖӮMiniMaxгҖҒжңҲд№Ӣжҡ—йқўгҖҒжҷәи°ұгҖҒDeepSeekеӣӣж¬ҫеӣҪдә§еӨ§жЁЎеһӢиҝӣе…Ҙе…Ёзҗғи°ғз”ЁйҮҸеүҚдә”гҖӮдёӯеӣҪдјҒдёҡи°ғз”ЁеӨ§жЁЎеһӢж—ҘеқҮиҫҫ37дёҮдәҝtokensпјҢйҳҝйҮҢдә‘еҚғй—®еҚ жҜ”з¬¬дёҖгҖӮ  
*жқҘжәҗпјҡеӨ®е№ҝзҪ‘гҖҒжҜҸж—Ҙз»ҸжөҺж–°й—»гҖҒиҜҒеҲёж—¶жҠҘгҖҒи…ҫи®ҜзҪ‘*  
*иҜ„еҲҶпјҡ90пјҲжқғеЁҒеӘ’дҪ“пјү+10пјҲеӨҡжәҗдәӨеҸүйӘҢиҜҒпјү+5пјҲж—¶ж•ҲжҖ§24hпјү= 105вҶ’100*

### 95еҲҶ
**е…Ёзҗғдәәе·ҘжҷәиғҪйўҶеҹҹ2жңҲеҸ‘еұ•зӣҳзӮ№пјҡжҠҖжңҜеҠ еҝ«иҗҪең°гҖҒиө„жң¬жҢҒз»ӯеҠ з ҒгҖҒжІ»зҗҶи®®йўҳеҚҮжё©**  
ж–°еҚҺзӨҫеҸ‘еёғз»јиҝ°пјҢ2жңҲе…ЁзҗғAIдә§дёҡе‘ҲзҺ°еҮәжҠҖжңҜеҠ еҝ«иҗҪең°гҖҒиө„жң¬жҢҒз»ӯеҠ з ҒгҖҒжІ»зҗҶи®®йўҳеҚҮжё©зҡ„жҖҒеҠҝгҖӮеүҚжІҝеӨ§жЁЎеһӢеҜҶйӣҶеҸ‘еёғпјҢеә”з”ЁеңәжҷҜеҠ йҖҹжӢ“еұ•гҖӮ  
*жқҘжәҗпјҡж–°еҚҺзӨҫ*  
*иҜ„еҲҶпјҡ90пјҲе®ҳж–№жқғеЁҒеӘ’дҪ“пјү+5пјҲж—¶ж•ҲжҖ§пјү= 95*

### 80еҲҶ
**ж¬§зӣҹжӯЈејҸж•Іе®ҡгҖҠдәәе·ҘжҷәиғҪжі•жЎҲгҖӢжңҖз»Ҳз«Ӣжі•ж–Үжң¬е№¶иҗҪең°е®һж–Ҫ**  
еҪ“ең°ж—¶й—ҙ2026е№ҙ2жңҲпјҢж¬§зӣҹжӯЈејҸйҖҡиҝҮгҖҠдәәе·ҘжҷәиғҪжі•жЎҲгҖӢпјҲEU AI ActпјүпјҢиў«з§°дёә"е…ЁзҗғйҰ–йғЁз»јеҗҲжҖ§дәәе·ҘжҷәиғҪзӣ‘з®Ўжі•и§„"гҖӮеӣӣзә§зӣ‘з®ЎдҪ“зі»пјҢ2027е№ҙе…Ёйқўз”ҹж•ҲгҖӮ  
*жқҘжәҗпјҡд»Ҡж—ҘеӨҙжқЎпјҲеј•з”Ёж¬§зӣҹе®ҳж–№е…¬е‘Ҡпјү*  
*иҜ„еҲҶпјҡ70пјҲиЎҢдёҡеӘ’дҪ“пјү+5пјҲе®ҳж–№е…¬е‘Ҡпјү+5пјҲж—¶ж•ҲжҖ§пјү= 80*

### 80еҲҶ
**AnthropicдёҺOpenAIеҗҢж—ҘеҸ‘еёғClaude Opus 4.6дёҺGPT-5.3-CodexпјҢAIиҝҲе…ҘиҮӘдё»жү§иЎҢж–°йҳ¶ж®ө**  
2026е№ҙ2жңҲ5ж—ҘпјҢдёӨеӨ§е…¬еҸёеҮ д№ҺеҗҢж—¶еҸ‘еёғж–°жЁЎеһӢгҖӮClaude Opus 4.6дё»жү“1MдёҠдёӢж–ҮзӘ—еҸЈгҖҒеӨҡAgentеҚҸдҪңе’ҢиҮӘйҖӮеә”жҖқиҖғпјҢдёҠдёӢж–ҮиғҪеҠӣжҸҗеҚҮ5еҖҚгҖӮGPT-5.3-Codexд»Һд»Јз ҒеҠ©жүӢиҝӣеҢ–дёәиғҪе®ҢжҲҗејҖеҸ‘иҖ…д»»дҪ•е·ҘдҪңзҡ„д»ЈзҗҶгҖӮ  
*жқҘжәҗпјҡCSDNгҖҒд»Ҡж—ҘеӨҙжқЎгҖҒзҪ‘жҳ“зӯү*  
*иҜ„еҲҶпјҡ70пјҲиЎҢдёҡеӘ’дҪ“пјү+10пјҲеӨҡжәҗжҠҘйҒ“пјү= 80*

### 80еҲҶ
**иҜәеҘ–еҫ—дё»DeepMind CEOжңҖж–°и®ҝи°Ҳпјҡи§ҶAIдёәжҷ®йҖҡжҠҖжңҜй”ҷиҜҜпјҢAGIиҝҳе·®2-3йЎ№йҮҚеӨ§еҲӣж–°**  
Demis HassabisеңЁжңҖж–°и®ҝи°ҲдёӯиЎЁзӨәпјҢе°ҶAIи§Ҷдёәжҷ®йҖҡжҠҖжңҜжҳҜй”ҷиҜҜзҡ„гҖӮд»–и®Өдёәи·қзҰ»AGIиҝҳйңҖиҰҒ2-3йЎ№йҮҚеӨ§еҲӣж–°пјҢеҗҢж—¶жҸҗеҲ°еҺ»е№ҙеҜ№DeepSeekзҡ„жҒҗжғ§иў«еӨёеӨ§дәҶпјҢдёӯеӣҪAIе…¬еҸё"еҸӘжҜ”еүҚжІҝж°ҙе№іиҗҪеҗҺ6дёӘжңҲ"гҖӮ  
*жқҘжәҗпјҡжқӯе·һж—ҘжҠҘгҖҒи…ҫи®ҜзҪ‘гҖҒCSDNзӯү*  
*иҜ„еҲҶпјҡ70пјҲиЎҢдёҡеӘ’дҪ“пјү+10пјҲеӨҡжәҗжҠҘйҒ“пјү= 80*

### 75еҲҶ
**DeepMind CEOпјҡд»Ҡе№ҙејҖеҗҜAIжҠ—зҷҢиҚҜзү©дёҙеәҠиҜ•йӘҢпјҢжңӘжқҘ18дёӘжңҲиҝҺжқҘжңәеҷЁдәәзӘҒз ҙ**  
Demis HassabisеңЁиҫҫжІғж–Ҝи®әеқӣдёҠиЎЁзӨәпјҢи°·жӯҢDeepMindе°ҶеңЁд»Ҡе№ҙеҗҜеҠЁAIжҠ—зҷҢиҚҜзү©дёҙеәҠиҜ•йӘҢпјҢжңӘжқҘ18дёӘжңҲе°ҶеңЁжңәеҷЁдәәйўҶеҹҹиҝҺжқҘзӘҒз ҙжҖ§иҝӣеұ•гҖӮ  
*жқҘжәҗпјҡжҗңзӢҗпјҲеј•з”ЁиҫҫжІғж–Ҝи®әеқӣеҸ‘иЁҖпјү*  
*иҜ„еҲҶпјҡ70пјҲиЎҢдёҡеӘ’дҪ“пјү+5пјҲе®ҳж–№еңәеҗҲеҸ‘иЁҖпјү= 75*

### 75еҲҶ
**з”°дё°пјҡ2026е№ҙAIеә”з”ЁзҲҶеҸ‘пјҢд»Һ"й«ҳеӨ§е…Ё"иө°еҗ‘"дё“зІҫзү№ж–°"**  
е•ҶжұӨжҷәиғҪдә§дёҡз ”з©¶йҷўйҷўй•ҝз”°дё°жҺҘеҸ—дё“и®ҝж—¶иЎЁзӨәпјҢ2026е№ҙе°ҶжҳҜAIеә”з”ЁзҲҶеҸ‘д№Ӣе№ҙпјҢAIеҸ‘еұ•е°Ҷд»Һ"й«ҳеӨ§е…Ё"иө°еҗ‘"дё“зІҫзү№ж–°"пјҢеһӮзӣҙйўҶеҹҹеә”з”Ёе°ҶжҲҗдёәйҮҚзӮ№гҖӮ  
*жқҘжәҗпјҡи…ҫи®ҜзҪ‘*  
*иҜ„еҲҶпјҡ70пјҲиЎҢдёҡеӘ’дҪ“пјү+5пјҲдё“е®¶и§ӮзӮ№пјү= 75*

### 75еҲҶ
**DeepMindеҸ‘еёғAGIж—¶й—ҙиЎЁпјҡиҝҳе·®2-3йЎ№йҮҚеӨ§еҲӣж–°**  
Demis HassabisеңЁиҫҫжІғж–Ҝдё–з•Ңз»ҸжөҺи®әеқӣдёҠйў„еҲӨAGIдҪ•ж—¶еҲ°жқҘпјҢиЎЁзӨәиҝҳйңҖиҰҒ2-3йЎ№йҮҚеӨ§еҲӣж–°гҖӮ  
*жқҘжәҗпјҡж–°жөӘиҙўз»Ҹзӯү*  
*иҜ„еҲҶпјҡ70пјҲиЎҢдёҡеӘ’дҪ“пјү+5пјҲе®ҳж–№еңәеҗҲпјү= 75*

### 70еҲҶ
**еҗҙжҒ©иҫҫгҖҒж–ҜеқҰзҰҸгҖҒи°·жӯҢдә‘зҪ•и§ҒеҗҢйў‘пјҡAIжөӢиҜ„йҖ»иҫ‘жӯЈиў«Agentйў иҰҶ**  
InfoQжҠҘйҒ“пјҢеҗҙжҒ©иҫҫгҖҒж–ҜеқҰзҰҸгҖҒи°·жӯҢдә‘дёүж–№зҪ•и§ҒиҫҫжҲҗе…ұиҜҶпјҡдј з»ҹзҡ„AIжөӢиҜ„йҖ»иҫ‘жӯЈеңЁиў«Agentйў иҰҶпјҢж–°зҡ„иҜ„дј°ж ҮеҮҶжӯЈеңЁеҪўжҲҗгҖӮ  
*жқҘжәҗпјҡInfoQ*  
*иҜ„еҲҶпјҡ70пјҲиЎҢдёҡеӘ’дҪ“пјү= 70*

### 70еҲҶ
**PalantirзӣҳеүҚжҡҙж¶ЁпјҒзҫҺеӣҪз©әиўӯдјҠжң—з«ҹеҖҹеҠ©Anthropicдәәе·ҘжҷәиғҪжҠҖжңҜ**  
еӘ’дҪ“жҠҘйҒ“з§°пјҢPalantirиӮЎд»·зӣҳеүҚж¶Ёиҝ‘4%пјҢиҫҫ142.6зҫҺе…ғпјҢдё»иҰҒеӣ зҫҺд»ҘиҒ”еҗҲз©әиўӯдјҠжң—иЎҢеҠЁдёӯдҪҝз”ЁдәҶAnthropicзҡ„дәәе·ҘжҷәиғҪжҠҖжңҜгҖӮ  
*жқҘжәҗпјҡAIbase*  
*иҜ„еҲҶпјҡ70пјҲиЎҢдёҡеӘ’дҪ“пјү= 70*

---

## еёӮеңәи®Ёи®әпјҲжңӘз»ҸиҜҒе®һ/зӨҫеҢәи®Ёи®әпјү

**зғӯй—ЁзӨҫеҢәиҜқйўҳ**пјҲжҢүзғӯеәҰжҺ’еәҸпјүпјҡ
1. Claude Opus 4.6 vs GPT-5.3-Codexпјҡи°ҒжүҚжҳҜзңҹжӯЈзҡ„иҮӘдё»жү§иЎҢд№ӢзҺӢпјҹрҹ”Ҙ
2. ж¬§зӣҹAIжі•жЎҲдјҡдёҚдјҡи®©ж¬§жҙІAIдә§дёҡжҺүйҳҹпјҹ  
3. дёӯеӣҪAIи°ғз”ЁйҮҸеҸҚи¶…зҫҺеӣҪпјҢжҳҜжҠҖжңҜзӘҒз ҙиҝҳжҳҜд»·ж јжҲҳпјҹ  
4. AGIеҲ°еә•иҝҳе·®д»Җд№ҲпјҹDeepMindзҡ„"2-3йЎ№еҲӣж–°"жҳҜд»Җд№Ҳпјҹ  
5. AnthropicжҠҖжңҜз”ЁдәҺеҶӣдәӢиЎҢеҠЁпјҢиҝҷдјҰзҗҶеә•зәҝжҳҜдёҚжҳҜз ҙдәҶпјҹ

---

## CжўҰзҡ„д»Ҡж—ҘжҜ’иҲҢ

дёӯеӣҪи°ғз”ЁйҮҸйҰ–и¶…зҫҺеӣҪпјҢиҝҷжҳҜдёҚжҳҜеӣ дёәдҪ дҫҝе®ңиҖҢдёҚжҳҜдҪ жӣҙејәпјҹеӣӣж¬ҫеӣҪдә§еӨ§жЁЎеһӢйңёжҰңеүҚдә”пјҢдҪҶз”ЁжҲ·еңЁз”Ёе®ғд»¬е№Ід»Җд№Ҳпјҹз”ҹжҲҗж–ҮжЎҲгҖҒеҶҷд»Јз ҒгҖҒиҝҳжҳҜиҒҠеӨ©пјҹ

Demis HassabisиҜҙдёӯеӣҪAIеҸӘиҗҪеҗҺ6дёӘжңҲгҖӮиҝҷжҳҜдёҚжҳҜеңЁеӨёеӨ§еҜ№жүӢпјҢиҝҳжҳҜзңҹзҡ„жңүиҝҷдёӘе·®и·қпјҹAGIиҝҳе·®2-3йЎ№йҮҚеӨ§еҲӣж–°пјҢжҳҜдёҚжҳҜж„Ҹе‘ізқҖзҺ°еңЁиҝҳеҸӘжҳҜзҺ©е…·пјҹ

ж¬§зӣҹAIжі•жЎҲз”ҹж•ҲпјҢиҝҷжҳҜдҝқжҠӨдәәзұ»пјҢиҝҳжҳҜжҠҠж¬§жҙІAIдә§дёҡе…іиҝӣз¬јеӯҗпјҹ

Claude Opus 4.6е’ҢGPT-5.3-CodexеҗҢж—¶еҸ‘еёғпјҢиҝҷжҳҜдёҚжҳҜиҜҙжҳҺз«һдәүе·Із»ҸзҷҪзғӯеҢ–еҲ°"жҠўйҰ–еҸ‘"зҡ„ең°жӯҘдәҶпјҹз”ЁжҲ·зңҹзҡ„йңҖиҰҒ1MдёҠдёӢж–ҮзӘ—еҸЈеҗ—пјҢиҝҳжҳҜиҝҷеҸӘжҳҜеҸӮж•°з«һиөӣпјҹ

дҪ жҳҜдёҚжҳҜиҝҷд№Ҳжғіпјҡ**жҠҖжңҜжҢҮж Ү вү  з”ЁжҲ·дҪ“йӘҢпјҢи°ғз”ЁйҮҸ вү  д»·еҖјеҲӣйҖ пјҢзӣ‘з®Ў вү  е®үе…Ё**пјҹ

---

## е…ій”®иҜҚжҖ»з»“

**жҠҖжңҜпјҡ** AgentиҮӘдё»жү§иЎҢгҖҒдёҠдёӢж–ҮзӘ—еҸЈгҖҒеӨҡAgentеҚҸдҪң  
**еёӮеңәпјҡ** дёӯеӣҪи°ғз”ЁйҮҸеҸҚи¶…зҫҺеӣҪгҖҒеӣҪдә§еӨ§жЁЎеһӢеүҚдә”гҖҒд»·ж јжҲҳ  
**зӣ‘з®Ўпјҡ** ж¬§зӣҹAIжі•жЎҲгҖҒеӣӣзә§зӣ‘з®ЎгҖҒеҶӣдәӢеә”з”ЁдјҰзҗҶ  
**жңӘжқҘпјҡ** AGIж—¶й—ҙиЎЁгҖҒжҠ—зҷҢиҚҜзү©дёҙеәҠиҜ•йӘҢгҖҒжңәеҷЁдәәзӘҒз ҙ

          - blockquote [ref=e52]: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance in language-centric tasks. However, in agentic settings, LLMs often struggle to anticipate action consequences and adapt to environment dynamics, highlighting the need for world-modeling capabilities in LLM-based agents. We propose Reinforcement World Model Learning (RWML), a self-supervised method that learns action-conditioned world models for LLM-based agents on textual states using sim-to-real gap rewards. We evaluate our method on ALFWorld and \tau^2 Bench and observe significant gains over the base model, despite being entirely self-supervised. When combined with task-success rewards, our method outperforms direct task-success reward RL by 6.9 and 5.7 points on ALFWorld and \tau^2 Bench respectively, while matching the performance of expert-data training.

  - text: arXiv:2602.05842v2 [cs.CL] 09 Feb 2026
  - article:
    - heading "Reinforcement World Model Learning for LLM-based Agents" [ref=e11] [level=1]
    - text: Xiao Yu Baolin Peng Ruize Xu Yelong Shen Pengcheng He Suman Nath Nikhil Singh Jiangfeng Gao Zhou Yu
    - heading "Abstract" [ref=e12] [level=6]
    - paragraph:
      - text: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance in language-centric tasks. However, in agentic settings, LLMs often struggle to anticipate action consequences and adapt to environment dynamics, highlighting the need for world-modeling capabilities in LLM-based agents. We propose Reinforcement World Model Learning (RWML), a self-supervised method that learns action-conditioned world models for LLM-based agents on textual states using sim-to-real gap rewards. Our method aligns simulated next states produced by the model with realized next states observed from the environment, encouraging consistency between internal world simulations and actual environment dynamics in a pre-trained embedding space. Unlike next-state token prediction, which prioritizes token-level fidelity (i.e., reproducing exact wording) over semantic equivalence and can lead to model collapse, our method provides a more robust training signal and is empirically less susceptible to reward hacking than LLM-as-a-judge. We evaluate our method on ALFWorld and
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench and observe significant gains over the base model, despite being entirely
      - emphasis: self-supervised
      - text: . When combined with task-success rewards, our method outperforms direct task-success reward RL by 6.9 and 5.7 points on ALFWorld and
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench respectively, while matching the performance of expert-data training.
    - text: Machine Learning, ICML
    - heading "1 Introduction" [ref=e13] [level=2]
    - paragraph:
      - text: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable success in a wide range of language-centric tasks, including question answering, code generation, and multi-step reasoning (Brown et al.,
      - link "2020" [ref=e14]:
      - text: ; Wei et al.,
      - link "2022" [ref=e15]:
      - text: ; Lample and Conneau,
      - link "2019" [ref=e16]:
      - text: ; RoziГЁre et al.,
      - link "2024" [ref=e17]:
      - text: ; DeepSeek-AI et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e18]:
      - text: ; OpenAI and et al.,
      - link "2024" [ref=e19] [nth=1]:
      - text: ). These advances have motivated growing interests in using LLMs as autonomous agents to interact with realistic environments and complete long-horizon tasks (Yao et al.,
      - link "2023" [ref=e20]:
      - text: ; Deng et al.,
      - link "2023" [ref=e21] [nth=1]:
      - text: ). Despite strong linguistic and reasoning abilities, LLM-based agents struggle in many agentic settings that require anticipating action consequences and adapting to environment dynamics (Liu et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e22] [nth=1]:
      - text: ). This discrepancy highlights the distinction between
      - emphasis: language competence
      - text: from pretraining and
      - emphasis: agentic intelligence
      - text: required for LLM-based agents.
    - 'figure "Figure 1: We propose RWML as a scalable, self-supervised method to improve the world modeling ability of LLM-based agent by learning from next-states, prior to downstream policy RL which learns from task-success reward."':
      - img "Refer to caption"
      - text: "Figure 1: We propose RWML as a scalable, self-supervised method to improve the world modeling ability of LLM-based agent by learning from next-states, prior to downstream policy RL which learns from task-success reward."
    - paragraph:
      - text: A key reason for this limitation is the misalignment between standard pretraining objectives and agentic use cases. Standard pretraining objectives such as next-token prediction over static text corpora emphasize language understanding and generation. In contrast, modern LLM-based agents operate in complex, long-horizon environments, where successful task completion requires reasoning about both the current state and how the environment might evolve in response to actions (LeCun,
      - link "2022" [ref=e23] [nth=1]:
      - text: ; Hu and Shu,
      - link "2023" [ref=e24] [nth=2]:
      - text: ; Hao et al.,
      - link "2023" [ref=e25] [nth=3]:
      - text: ). The ability to model potential future outcomes of oneвҖҷs actions is central to biological intelligence. Research in neuroscience and psychology shows that humans, animals, and intelligent systems use internal world models to reason, plan, explore, and learn efficiently from very few trials (Craik,
      - link "1944" [ref=e26]:
      - text: ; Tolman,
      - link "1948" [ref=e27]:
      - text: ; Daw et al.,
      - link "2005" [ref=e28]:
      - text: ; Daw and Dayan,
      - link "2014" [ref=e29]:
      - text: ; Bennett,
      - link "2023" [ref=e30] [nth=4]:
      - text: ). We believe this capacity for world modeling is likewise essential for effective reasoning and planning in LLM-based agents.
    - paragraph:
      - text: Recent work has explored equipping LLM-based agents with world-modeling capabilities, training LLMs to predict next-states using next-token prediction (i.e., SFT). Examples include Zhang et al. (
      - link "2025a" [ref=e31]:
      - text: ); Yu et al. (
      - link "2025c" [ref=e32]:
      - text: ") which teaches LLMs to model environment transitions using trajectories provided by expert policies or high-quality synthetic data generated with stronger language models. While effective in some settings, these methods face scalability challenges: (1) they rely heavily on high-quality data from experts/strong LLMs; and (2) they are based on SFT, which prioritizes token-level fidelity (i.e., reproducing exact wording) over semantic equivalence and can lead to model collapse."
    - paragraph:
      - text: In this paper, we propose Reinforcement World Model Learning (RWML), a self-supervised training method based on RL that learns action-conditioned world models for LLM-based agents. Rather than optimizing token-level fidelity with SFT, RWML trains LLMs to minimize the discrepancy between simulated next states produced by the model and realized next states observed from the environment, measured in a pre-trained embedding space. This sim-to-real alignment promotes semantic consistency between the agentвҖҷs internal world model and real environment dynamics while preserving task-relevant transitions, making them suitable for downstream decision-making. We evaluate our method on two long-horizon agent benchmarks (ALFWorld and
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench), and find RWML significantly improved the base model performance by 19.6 and 6.9 points without using any expert data, strong LLMs, or task-success reward signal. When combined with task-success rewards, agents trained with RWML outperform direct task-success reward RL by 6.9 and 5.7 points on ALFWorld and
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench, respectively, while matching the performance of training with expert data.
    - paragraph:
      - text: "In summary, our contributions are: (1) We propose RWML as a scalable, self-supervised training method for LLM-based agents that learns action-conditioned world models from sim-to-real gap rewards. (2) We evaluate our method on two long-horizon benchmarks (ALFWorld and"
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench) and find that RWML significantly improves base model performance. When combined with task-success rewards, our models outperform standard RL and match the performance of training with expert data. (3) We conduct comprehensive analyses вҖ” including ablation studies, model forgetting, qualitative analysis, and more вҖ” to highlight the benefits of RL and world model learning for LLM-based agents.

We then train ПҖ Оё to reason as a world model via GRPO, using lightweight reward functions (e.g., embedding-based cosine similarity) to compare the predicted s ^ t + 1 with the real s t + 1 ."':

    - heading "2 Method" [ref=e34] [level=2]
    - heading "2.1 Notation" [ref=e35] [level=3]

    - heading "2.2 Reinforcement World Model Learning" [ref=e40] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: A key challenge in scaling agentic post-training methods such as RL is their reliance on accurate task-success rewards provided at the end of an episode. While effective, these rewards are sparse and require careful design by domain experts (Barres et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e41] [nth=3]:
      - text: ; Xie et al.,
      - link "2024" [ref=e42] [nth=2]:
      - text: ; Rawles et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e43] [nth=4]:
      - text: ; Zhou et al.,
      - link "2024" [ref=e44] [nth=3]:
      - text: ). As tasks and environments become more complex, this reliance introduces scaling challenges.
    - paragraph:
      - text: We introduce Reinforcement World Model Learning (RWML), a scalable, self-supervised training method where the agent learns accurate world model knowledge from the environment dynamics
      - math: рқ’Ҝ
      - text: ", before further finetuning with task-success reward RL. Intuitively, RWML trains an LLM policy"
      - math: ПҖ Оё
      - text: to
      - emphasis: also be able to reason about the consequences
      - math: s ^ t + 1
      - text: given an action

    - heading "3 Experiments" [ref=e52] [level=2]
    - paragraph: We evaluate RWML on two widely used long-horizon environments that require accurate world and tool understanding for effective planning and task completion.
    - heading "3.1 Experiment Setup" [ref=e53] [level=3]
    - heading "Benchmarks" [ref=e54] [level=4]
    - paragraph:
      - text: We conduct experiments on two popular agent benchmarks, ALFWorld (Shridhar et al.,
      - link "2021" [ref=e55] [nth=1]:
      - text: ) and
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench (Barres et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e56] [nth=7]:
      - text: ). ALFWorld is a text-based embodied environment where the agent needs to locate and interact with objects to complete household tasks using natural language instructions.
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench is an interleaved tool-use environment where the model acts as a customer service agent and uses tool-calls to resolve issues while conversing to a simulated user who raised the issue. We use the official training and test splits provided by each benchmark for training and evaluation.
    - heading "Baselines" [ref=e57] [level=4]
    - paragraph:
      - text: "We compare with other policy and world model related training methods from three categories: (1) learning from task-success reward; (2) learning from interaction/transition function"
      - math: рқ’Ҝ
      - text: ", similar to our method; and (3) learning from expert annotations/stronger LLMs."
    - list:
      - listitem:
      - listitem:
      - listitem:
    - paragraph:
      - text: In addition to these training-based methods, we also evaluate ReACT-style prompting (Yao et al.,
      - link "2023" [ref=e58] [nth=5]:
      - text: ) on closed-source LLMs such as GPT-5 (Singh et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e59] [nth=8]:
      - text: ) as additional references. For a more high-level comparison between these methods and our approach, please see
      - link "TableЛңA1" [ref=e60]:
      - text: .

    - heading "Models and Training Data" [ref=e62] [level=4]
    - paragraph:
      - text: Following prior work (Feng et al.,
      - link "2025c" [ref=e63] [nth=1]:
      - text: ; Yu et al.,
      - link "2025a" [ref=e64] [nth=2]:
      - text: ; Zhang et al.,
      - link "2025a" [ref=e65] [nth=3]:
      - text: ), we train from Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (Qwen et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e66] [nth=9]:
      - text: ) on ALFWorld for all methods. On
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench, we train from Qwen3-8B (Yang et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e67] [nth=10]:
      - text: ) for all methods, due to the difficulty of the benchmark and the enhanced tool-use capabilities from Qwen3 models.
    - paragraph:
      - text: For RWML, we collect interaction data using
      - math: ПҖ Оё
      - text: to rollout
      - math: "N"
      - text: trajectories per training task with temperature

      - text: ", with"
      - math: N = 3
      - text: for ALFWorld and
      - math: N = 6
      - text: for
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench. Then, we split all turns into triplets of
      - math: вҹЁ s вүӨ t , a t , s t + 1 вҹ©
      - text: for all
      - math: t
      - text: ", using 90% of the triplets for training and 10% for validation. Finally, we subsample вҖңsimpleвҖқ training samples using a"
      - math: П„ easy
      - text: that corresponds to
      - math: вҲј
      - text: 30% of the training data for both benchmarks. In contrast to our baselines, we note that the entire process does not require any expert annotation/stronger LLMs nor require task success/failure signals. Only triplets of
      - math: вҹЁ s вүӨ t , a t , s t + 1 вҹ©
      - text: are required.
    - paragraph:
      - text: Finally, for Policy RL training, we use GRPO to let the model learn to solve the tasks using task-success rewards with
      - math: Оі = 1.0

    - heading "3.2 Main Results" [ref=e73] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: In
      - link "TableЛң1" [ref=e74]:
      - text: we demonstrate the effectiveness of RWML as a self-supervised method, trained solely from interaction data. Without using any expert data, strong LLMs, or task-success reward signals, RWML significantly improved agentic capability compared to the base model, advancing 19.6 and 7.9 points on ALFWorld and
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench, respectively. When combined with task-success reward (i.e., Policy RL), we find our models outperform all other training-based baselines. Notably, in
      - link "TableЛң2" [ref=e75]:
      - text: we find (1) on ALFWorld, our models even outperform approaches that use expert annotations/strong LLMs; and (2) on
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench, our models achieve the second best overall score, despite not accessing any expert data/strong LLMs. This demonstrates the effectiveness of RWML, whose scalable, self-supervised design represents a promising direction for вҖңmid-trainingвҖқ algorithms that can complement post-training methods such as Policy RL to further improve LLM-based agent performance.
    - heading "3.3 RWML Forgets Less" [ref=e76] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: In
      - link "TableЛң3" [ref=e77]:
      - text: ", we evaluate relative susceptibility of RL and SFT to catastrophic forgetting (Kirkpatrick et al.,"
      - link "2017" [ref=e78]:
      - text: ; Luo et al.,
      - link "2025c" [ref=e79] [nth=2]:
      - text: ) in the context of world model learning. We evaluate our models trained on ALFWorld and
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench on (1) general knowledge benchmarks such as MMLU-Redux (Gema et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e80] [nth=12]:
      - text: ) and IFEval (Zhou et al.,
      - link "2023" [ref=e81] [nth=6]:
      - text: ); (2) math and STEM problems such as MATH-500 (Lightman et al.,
      - link "2023" [ref=e82] [nth=7]:
      - text: ), GSM8k (Cobbe et al.,
      - link "2021" [ref=e83] [nth=2]:
      - text: ), and GPQA-Diamond (Rein et al.,
      - link "2023" [ref=e84] [nth=8]:
      - text: ); and (3) coding tasks such as LiveCodeBench (Jain et al.,
      - link "2024" [ref=e85] [nth=6]:
      - text: ). In
      - link "TableЛң3" [ref=e86] [nth=1]:
      - text: ", we find RWML leads to less model forgetting compared to WM SFT on nearly all benchmarks. We believe this is consistent with findings from prior work (Shenfeld et al.,"
      - link "2025" [ref=e87] [nth=13]:
      - text: ; Chen et al.,
      - link "2025a" [ref=e88] [nth=5]:
      - text: ), that online RL preserves prior knowledge and capabilities significantly better than SFT due to its on-policy nature. For more analysis on model parameter updates, please see
      - link "SectionЛң4.2" [ref=e89]:
      - text: .
    - heading "3.4 Ablation Studies" [ref=e90] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: In
      - link "TableЛң4" [ref=e91]:
      - text: "we present an ablation study to investigate the contribution of different components in our RWML. Specifically, we consider: (1) replacing our embedding-based reward with LLM-as-a-judge (Zheng et al.,"
      - link "2023" [ref=e92] [nth=9]:
      - text: "); (2) removing the data subsampling step which subsamples вҖңtoo easyвҖқ samples, denoted as вҖңw/o subsampleвҖқ; (3) removing the RWML training entirely, denoted as вҖңw/o trainingвҖқ. For LLM-as-a-judge, we consider two variants: prompting the LLM to compare the generated"
      - math: s ^ t + 1
      - text: with the ground truth
      - math: s t + 1
      - text: and return a
      - emphasis: real-valued reward
      - math: r вҲҲ [ 0 , 1 ]
      - text: ", allowing for partial credits. We denote this as вҖңw/ LLM-as-a-judgeвҖқ. Alternatively, we prompt the LLM to return a"
      - emphasis: binary reward
      - text: of either 0.0 or 1.0. We denote this as вҖңw/ bin(LLM-as-a-judge)вҖқ. In both cases, we use Qwen-3-235B-A22B-Instruct (Yang et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e93] [nth=14]:
      - text: ) as the judge model as it is a fast, strong, open-source LLM that can be hosted locally.
    - paragraph:
      - text: Results in
      - link "TableЛң4" [ref=e94] [nth=1]:
      - text: show that all components of our method are important in improving model performance. Additionally, we find that (1) weaker models such as Qwen2.5-7B are more susceptible to data quality/noisy reward functions; (2) LLM-as-a-judge is unreliable and can sometimes be hacked during training (see
      - link "AppendixЛңD" [ref=e95]:
      - text: for an example); and (3) subsampling вҖңeasyвҖқ training samples is beneficial to further improve model performance.

    - heading "4.1 Impact of RWML on Decision-Making" [ref=e99] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: In this section, we provide some qualitative and quantitative analyses of modelвҖҷs decision-making behavior before and after RWML training. Qualitatively, in
      - link "FigureЛң5" [ref=e100]:
      - text: we find RWML-trained models produce more accurate and efficient decisions, utilizing its improved knowledge about the environment. For example, in ALFWorld, our model correctly predicts that a вҖңknifeвҖқ is most likely on вҖңcountertopвҖқ rather than other locations, completing the task within 5 steps. In
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench, it correctly considers the possibility that the airplane mode is on вҖ” a case omitted by the base model.
    - paragraph:
      - text: Quantitatively, we find RWML effectively mitigates generating invalid/ineffective actions on both benchmarks, despite not being explicitly trained to do so. On ALFWorld, the proportion of invalid (e.g., formatting errors) or inefficient actions (e.g., вҖңlookвҖқ and вҖңexamineвҖқ actions) drops from 59.30% to 39.45% after RWML. Similarly, on
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench, the proportion of invalid tool calls (e.g., made-up tool names or incorrect arguments) decreases from 24.90% to 8.84% per tool-call made. Overall, our qualitative and quantitative results demonstrate that RWML meaningfully improves the decision-making ability of an LLM in agentic environments.
    - 'figure "Figure 4: RWML training with different base models on П„ 2 Bench."':
      - img "Refer to caption"
      - text: "Figure 4: RWML training with different base models on"
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench.
    - heading "4.2 Weight Change Analysis" [ref=e101] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: To understand the effectiveness of RWML, we also analyze how it reshapes model parameters during training. Following Zhu et al. (
      - link "2025" [ref=e102] [nth=15]:
      - text: ), we examine parameter-wise weight changes relative to the untrained base model, adopting the same definition and threshold
      - math: О· = 10 вҲ’ 3
      - text: "to identify major point-wise updates:"
    - table:
      - rowgroup:
    - paragraph:
      - text: where
      - math: w i , w i ^ вҲҲ в„қ
      - text: are finite, non-zero scalars of modelsвҖҷ weight points before and after tuning.
    - paragraph:
      - text: For each layer, we compute the ratio of parameters that undergo major updates. Results for Qwen3-8B on
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: "-Bench and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct on ALFWorld are shown in"
      - link "FigureЛң3" [ref=e103]:
      - text: . Full results are in
      - link "AppendixЛңE" [ref=e104]:
      - text: . A consistent pattern emerges that RWML induces notably fewer parameter changes across layers compared to WM SFT, indicating that it encodes task-relevant information with a smaller and more targeted set of updates (also see
      - link "AppendixЛңE" [ref=e105] [nth=1]:
      - text: ). This suggests that RWML learns in a more parameter-efficient and structurally conservative manner, avoiding widespread modifications to the pretrained representation space.
    - paragraph:
      - text: Importantly, this compact update behavior also help explain why RWML integrates well with subsequent policy learning, as shown in
      - link "FigureЛң3" [ref=e106] [nth=1]:
      - text: . When followed by Policy RL, the resulting weight-change ratios remain remarkably close to those of Policy RL applied directly to the base model. In contrast, models initialized with WM SFT exhibit substantially higher change ratios after policy optimization, reflecting stronger parametric interference. These observations suggest that RWML maintains a parameter landscape more compatible with policy learning, reducing conflict and redundancy during post-training.
    - paragraph:
      - text: Overall, we find this parameter update behavior of RWML is consistent across both benchmarks and largely invariant to different transformer components, including attention (Q/K/V/O) and MLP projection layers (see
      - link "AppendixЛңE" [ref=e107] [nth=2]:
      - text: ). The consistently lower change ratio of RWML-trained models compared to that of WM SFT also aligns with our findings in
      - link "SectionЛң3.3" [ref=e108] [nth=1]:
      - text: ", which shows that RWML better mitigates catastrophic forgetting. These results provide a perspective distinct from the conventional SFT-then-RL paradigm: applying RL in both вҖңmid-trainingвҖқ and post-training stages appears to produce more stable and consistent parameter updates, and may help explain the improved performance."
    - heading "4.3 Impact of Base Model Capability" [ref=e109] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: On the challenging
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: bench, we find the ability to learn and transfer world model knowledge from RWML to decision-making is dependent on the capability of the base model. In
      - link "FigureЛң4" [ref=e110]:
      - text: ", we perform RWML training with three different base models: Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3-8B, and Qwen3-30B-A3B"
      - superscript: "3"
      - text: . We find that weaker models like Qwen2.5-7B struggle to transfer world knowledge to decision-making on the challenging
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench, while stronger models (Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-30B-A3B) show substantial gains, approaching the performance of Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507. This suggests that RWML is most effective for (sufficiently) strong base models. We leave improving transfer abilities for weaker models to future work.
    - 'figure "Figure 5: After RWML, models produce more accurate and efficient decisions by leveraging its improved knowledge of the environment."':
      - img "Refer to caption"
      - text: "Figure 5: After RWML, models produce more accurate and efficient decisions by leveraging its improved knowledge of the environment."
    - heading "5 Related Work" [ref=e111] [level=2]
    - heading "Training Decision-Making Agents" [ref=e112] [level=4]
    - paragraph:
      - text: LLM-based agents (Yao et al.,
      - link "2023" [ref=e113] [nth=10]:
      - text: ; Shinn et al.,
      - link "2023" [ref=e114] [nth=11]:
      - text: ) has seen wide applications in domains such as interactive gaming (Wang et al.,
      - link "2023" [ref=e115] [nth=12]:
      - text: ; Feng et al.,
      - link "2025c" [ref=e116] [nth=3]:
      - text: ); software engineering (Jimenez et al.,
      - link "2024" [ref=e117] [nth=8]:
      - text: ; Yang et al.,
      - link "2024" [ref=e118] [nth=9]:
      - text: ); computer, phone, browser-use (Xie et al.,
      - link "2024" [ref=e119] [nth=10]:
      - text: ; Rawles et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e120] [nth=16]:
      - text: ; Zhou et al.,
      - link "2024" [ref=e121] [nth=11]:
      - text: ; Yu et al.,
      - link "2025b" [ref=e122] [nth=1]:
      - text: ), and more. Many early work on training language agents primarily rely on imitation learning (i.e., SFT), using either demonstrations from human experts (Deng et al.,
      - link "2023" [ref=e123] [nth=13]:
      - text: ; Chen et al.,
      - link "2025b" [ref=e124] [nth=2]:
      - text: ; Wang et al.,
      - link "2025a" [ref=e125] [nth=6]:
      - text: ) or trajectories synthesized from stronger LLMs often accompanied with a set of manually designed workflows/heuristics (Zeng et al.,
      - link "2023" [ref=e126] [nth=14]:
      - text: ; Chen et al.,
      - link "2024" [ref=e127] [nth=12]:
      - text: ; Su et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e128] [nth=17]:
      - text: ; Xu et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e129] [nth=18]:
      - text: ). While high-quality SFT data offers dense supervision signals, it is difficult to scale due to the high cost of collecting such demonstrations. Alternatively, recent efforts in RL bypasses the need for step-by-step demonstrations and instead directly learn from terminal rewards (i.e., task success) through trail and error. Recent work include Feng et al. (
      - link "2025a" [ref=e130] [nth=7]:
      - text: ); Tan et al. (
      - link "2025" [ref=e131] [nth=19]:
      - text: ); Luo et al. (
      - link "2025a" [ref=e132] [nth=8]:
      - text: ); Jin et al. (
      - link "2025" [ref=e133] [nth=20]:
      - text: ); Wang et al. (
      - link "2025b" [ref=e134] [nth=3]:
      - text: ), often powered by algorithms such as PPO (Schulman et al.,
      - link "2017" [ref=e135] [nth=1]:
      - text: ) and GRPO (Shao et al.,
      - link "2024" [ref=e136] [nth=13]:
      - text: ). However, designing task-success reward functions in complex environments still requires substantial human expertise (Chowdhury et al.,
      - link "2024" [ref=e137] [nth=14]:
      - text: ; Xie et al.,
      - link "2024" [ref=e138] [nth=15]:
      - text: ; Gou et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e139] [nth=21]:
      - text: ), limiting scalability. Together, these works motivate the need for more scalable training methods to bridge the gap between next-token-prediction pretrained models and their downstream applications in long-horizon agentic environments.
    - heading "Training World Models" [ref=e140] [level=4]
    - paragraph:
      - text: Beyond task-success rewards, real-world interaction data contains rich information that can be used to help decision-making. Early examples include Dyna algorithms (Sutton,
      - link "1991" [ref=e141]:
      - text: ) which separately trains a world model to combine model-based with model-free learning for efficient policy training. Recent applications on LLM agents either train a
      - emphasis: separate
      - text: world model to support inference-time algorithms such as MCTS (Hao et al.,
      - link "2023" [ref=e142] [nth=15]:
      - text: ; Wu et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e143] [nth=22]:
      - text: ; Chae et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e144] [nth=23]:
      - text: ; Gu et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e145] [nth=24]:
      - text: ), or jointly learn world models and policies within a single model to improve generalization (FAIR CodeGen team et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e146] [nth=25]:
      - text: ; Zhang et al.,
      - link "2025a" [ref=e147] [nth=9]:
      - text: ; Yu et al.,
      - link "2025a" [ref=e148] [nth=10]:
      - text: ","
      - link "c" [ref=e149]:
      - text: ; Feng et al.,
      - link "2025b" [ref=e150] [nth=4]:
      - text: ; Li et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e151] [nth=26]:
      - text: ; Qian et al.,
      - link "2026" [ref=e152]:
      - text: ). However, these approaches either require expensive training/inference of multiple models, or rely on additional annotations from experts/strong LLMs during world model learning. We propose RWML as a scalable,
      - emphasis: self-supervised
      - text: method to improve the world knowledge and decision-making ability of a single model.
    - heading "6 Conclusion" [ref=e153] [level=2]
    - paragraph:
      - text: We propose RWML, a scalable, self-supervised method that enhances the environment understanding and decision-making ability of LLM-based agents prior to downstream RL with task-success reward. Without expert/strong LLM annotations or task-success signals, RWML trains the LLM as an action-conditioned world model by aligning the simulated next states with observed environment states in a pre-trained embedding space. We evaluate RWML on two long-horizon agent benchmarks, ALFWorld and
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench, and find significant performance gains while using only interaction data. When combined with task-success rewards in policy RL, our method outperforms direct policy RL on both benchmarks and matches training with expert data. We believe our work opens up new avenues for scalable, self-supervised training methods to further advance LLM-based agents in the era of agentic RL.
    - heading "7 Impact Statements" [ref=e154] [level=2]
    - paragraph:
      - text: This paper presents work that aims to advance the agentic capabilities of LLM-based agents through a scalable, self-supervised method. While most LLM-based agent methods are not designed for unethical use, their applications and data collection processes may still pose risks of misuse. In this work, we propose RWML, which improves world modeling in LLM-based agents using interaction data without expert annotations or stronger LLMs, and is trained exclusively on established, isolated benchmarks without real-world impact. We believe that developing guardrails, such as safety filters (OpenAI,
      - link "2022" [ref=e155] [nth=2]:
      - text: ; Inan et al.,
      - link "2023" [ref=e156] [nth=16]:
      - text: ; Luo et al.,
      - link "2025b" [ref=e157] [nth=5]:
      - text: ), and using isolated environments like sandboxes (AgentInfra Team,
      - link "2025" [ref=e158] [nth=27]:
      - text: ; Pan et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e159] [nth=28]:
      - text: ), is essential for safe AI agent research. We do not condone the use of RWML or its constituent methods for any unlawful or morally unjust purposes.

    - heading "Appendix A LLM Usage" [ref=e161] [level=2]
    - paragraph: This work used LLMs as general-purpose writing assistants to improve the grammar and clarity of the paper. We did not use LLMs to generate any research ideas, automate experiments, or analyze results.

    - heading "Appendix B More Details on ALFWorld" [ref=e162] [level=2]
    - paragraph:
      - text: ALFWorld (Shridhar et al.,
      - link "2021" [ref=e163] [nth=3]:
      - text: ) is a text-based, long-horizon agent environment designed to align with the embodied ALFRED benchmark (Shridhar et al.,
      - link "2021" [ref=e164] [nth=4]:
      - text: ). An ALFWorld task can involve over 50 locations and require more than 50 steps for an expert policy, challenging agents to plan, track subgoals, and explore these locations efficiently. In particular, a key challenge in ALFWorld is identifying likely locations of household items (e.g., desklamps are likely on desks, shelves, or dressers). This makes ALFWorld well suited for evaluating both pretrained commonsense and learned world knowledge of an LLM-based agent.
    - heading "B.1 RWML Training Setup" [ref=e165] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: To collect training data for RWML (and WM SFT), we use the target model (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) to rollout
      - math: N = 3
      - text: trajectories per training task with temperature
      - math: П„ = 1.0
      - text: . We use 2048 tasks from the ALFWorld original training set with a maximum step of 30. We then convert these rollouts to triplets of
      - math: вҹЁ s вүӨ t , a t , s t + 1 вҹ©
      - text: . After minor postprocessing (e.g., removing some samples that contains invalid actions), we obtain 21,011 triplets for training and 2,288 for validation. Then, we finetune a filtering model with SFT on the validation split, and subsampled вҖңtoo easyвҖқ training samples with
      - math: П„ d = 0.1 , П„ easy = 0.0
      - text: which corresponds to
      - math: вҲј
      - text: 30% of the original training data. We set
      - math: p = 0.1
      - text: to subsample these вҖңtoo easyвҖқ training samples, so that the final dataset retains mostly medium-to-hard examples while preserving sufficient dataset diversity. We note that this value is chosen heuristically without tuning and is also fixed for
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench (see
      - link "SectionЛңB.1" [ref=e166]:
      - text: ). An overview of hyperparameter heuristics/intuitions is shown in
      - link "TableЛңA2" [ref=e167]:
      - text: . This results in a final training set of 15,813 triplets. For a fair comparison, both RWML and WM SFT are then trained on this final training set.
    - paragraph:
      - text: For
      - math: r WM
      - text: during training, we using Qwen3-Embedding-8B (Zhang et al.,
      - link "2025b" [ref=e168] [nth=6]:
      - text: ) with
      - math: П„ d = 0.2
      - text: . For RWML, we prompt the model to generate reasoning before making a final prediction of the next state. For WM SFT, we directly train the model to predict the next state with empty reasoning tokens. Since there is no reasoning data available for the triplets, we find this training method for WM SFT can better enable generalization/reasoning during the second stage Policy RL training. We present the prompts used for RWML and WM SFT in
      - link "TableЛңA4" [ref=e169]:
      - text: and
      - link "TableЛңA5" [ref=e170]:
      - text: ", respectively. For RWML, we train over 2 epochs using a learning rate of 1e-6, batch size of 32, group size of 8 with 2xB200 GPUs. For WM SFT, we train over 2 epochs using a learning rate of 2e-6, effective batch size of 32 over 4xB200 GPUs."
    - heading "B.2 Policy RL Training Setup" [ref=e171] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: For Policy RL, we mainly follow setups and prompts from Feng et al. (
      - link "2025c" [ref=e172] [nth=4]:
      - text: ); Yu et al. (
      - link "2025a" [ref=e173] [nth=11]:
      - text: ). We use the official training split from ALFWorld during training, prompting the model to generate reasoning tokens (i.e., <think>вҖҰ</think>) before generating an action. We use a maximum step of 15 during training, using
      - math: Оі = 1.0
      - text: to propagate terminal task success rewards to every turn in the trajectory. We note that this setup is identical to all Policy RL experiments (e.g., RWML+Policy RL and WM SFT+Policy RL). The only differences is the starting model checkpoint. All Policy RL runs are trained with GRPO over 300 steps with a group size of 8 on 2xB200 GPUs. Average training time is 28 hours.
    - heading "B.3 Other Training Setup" [ref=e174] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: For Imitation Learning, IWM, and SR, we use expert data from the official ALFWorld dataset, following Zhang et al. (
      - link "2025a" [ref=e175] [nth=12]:
      - text: ). For reflection data in SR, we follow Zhang et al. (
      - link "2025a" [ref=e176] [nth=13]:
      - text: ) and use a branching factor of 3 per expert action. Then, we use the prompt provided in Zhang et al. (
      - link "2025a" [ref=e177] [nth=14]:
      - text: ) to generate reflection data with the target model (i.e., Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct). However, we find that the target model cannot consistently generate coherent reflections, likely due to its limited long-context instruction-following capability. Therefore, following Yu et al. (
      - link "2025c" [ref=e178] [nth=5]:
      - text: ), we use a stronger LLM (i.e., GPT-4o) to generate reflections. After data collection, we follow Zhang et al. (
      - link "2025a" [ref=e179] [nth=15]:
      - text: ) and performed SFT training by combining the world model data/critic data with the original expert actions dataset. All training is done on 4xB200 GPUs, similar to our WM SFT.
    - heading "Appendix C More Details on Bench" [ref=e180] [level=2]:
      - text: Appendix C More Details on
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench
    - paragraph:
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench (Barres et al.,
      - link "2025" [ref=e181] [nth=29]:
      - text: ) is a text-based, long-horizon agent environment designed to evaluate the customer service ability of an LLM-based agent in a dual-control environment, where both the agent and user can make use of tool calls to act in a shared, dynamic environment. A
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench task, in practice, requires the agent to communicate with the user to gather information, make tool-calls, and adapt to the evolving environment state (e.g., users making tool-calls). Tasks spans across domains such as telecom, retail, and airline support, creating diverse multi-step interactions that challenge agents to plan, adapt, and coordinate with the user. In particular, solving
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench tasks requires reasoning about which tool to use (e.g., what information or actions each tool provides) as well as modeling user intent and behavior. This makes
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench suitable for evaluating the tool/user understanding and modeling ability of an LLM-based agent.
    - heading "C.1 RWML Training Setup" [ref=e182] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: To collect training data for RWML (and WM SFT), we use the target model (Qwen3-8B) to rollout
      - math: N total = 6
      - text: trajectories per training task. Specifically, since
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench has limited training samples (178 tasks in the training split), we performed rollout with
      - math: N = 3
      - text: using GPT-4.1 as the user simulator and
      - math: N = 3
      - text: using Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct as the user simulator to promote diversity. We then converted all rollouts to triplets of
      - math: вҹЁ s вүӨ t , a t , s t + 1 вҹ©
      - text: ". To prevent the model from memorizing database values in the tool responses (e.g.,{\"customer_id\": \"abc123\", \"full_name\": \"John Doe\"}), we masked these values by converting them to the corresponding OpenAPI schema (e.g., {\"type\": \"object\", \"properties\": {\"customer_id\": {\"type\": \"string\"}, \"full_name\": {\"type\": \"string\"}}}). Similarly, to prevent memorization of user details, we also provide basic user information available to the user simulator (e.g., вҖңI am John Doe. My phone number is 123-456-7890.вҖқ) in the prompt. We do not provide information regarding the userвҖҷs intent in the world model learning prompts. We present an example in"
      - link "TableЛңA6" [ref=e183]:
      - text: and
      - link "TableЛңA7" [ref=e184]:
      - text: . Then, we follow
      - link "SectionЛңB.1" [ref=e185] [nth=1]:
      - text: to subsample вҖңtoo easyвҖқ triplets with
      - math: П„ d = 0.15 , П„ easy = 0.0
      - text: and obtained 5,578 triplets for training. We set
      - math: p = 0.1
      - text: to subsample these вҖңtoo easyвҖқ training samples, so that the final dataset retains mostly medium-to-hard examples while preserving sufficient dataset diversity. An overview of hyperparameter heuristics/intuitions is show in
      - link "TableЛңA2" [ref=e186] [nth=1]:
      - text: . In total,
      - math: вҲј
      - text: 60% of the
      - math: s t + 1
      - text: are tool-use responses and
      - math: вҲј
      - text: 40% are user responses.
    - paragraph:
      - text: For
      - math: r WM
      - text: during training, we use Qwen3-Embedding-8B (Zhang et al.,
      - link "2025b" [ref=e187] [nth=7]:
      - text: ) similar to
      - link "SectionЛңB.1" [ref=e188] [nth=2]:
      - text: . However, since tool-use responses are generally structured outputs, we find using rouge-score (Lin,
      - link "2004" [ref=e189]:
      - text: ) is more effective at capturing these structures and any missing keys/values. Our final reward function in
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: "Bench is defined as:"

For WM SFT, we train over 2 epochs using a learning rate of 2e-6 and an effective batch size of 32 over 4xB200 GPUs."
    - heading "C.2 Policy RL Training Setup" [ref=e190] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: We extend the codebase from
      - link "SectionЛңB.2" [ref=e191]:
      - text: to allow for multi-turn rollouts with
      - math: t a u 2
      - text: Bench. We use the official prompts from

      - math: Оі = 1.0
      - text: to propagate terminal task success rewards to every turn in the trajectory. All Policy RL runs are trained with GRPO over 200 steps with a group size of 8 on 8xB200 GPUs. Average training time is 5 days.

      - text: "Table A3: Performance on"
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench using the official evaluation setting (GPT-4.1 as user simulator, and a maximum step of 100).
      - table:
    - heading "C.3 Additional Evaluation Results" [ref=e192] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: To augment our evaluation in
      - link "TableЛң1" [ref=e193] [nth=1]:
      - text: and
      - link "TableЛң2" [ref=e194] [nth=1]:
      - text: ", we additionally evaluate our models using the official setting with GPT-4.1 as user simulator and a maximum step of 100. Since GPT-4.1 is expensive, we evaluate the representative models in each category of"
      - link "TableЛң1" [ref=e195] [nth=2]:
      - text: and
      - link "TableЛң2" [ref=e196] [nth=2]:
      - text: . We present the results in
      - link "TableЛңA3" [ref=e197]:
      - text: . In general, we find our method surpasses all training methods that does not use experts/strong LLMs; and is competitive compared to methods that use experts/strong LLMs.
    - heading "C.4 Other Training Setup" [ref=e198] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: Imitation Learning, IWM, and SR requires expert rollouts for training. However, since
      - math: П„ 2
      - text: Bench does not provide expert trajectory annotations, we collect вҖңexpertвҖқ rollouts using rejection sampling with a strong LLM. Specifically, we use Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507 and perform rollouts with
      - math: N total = 6
      - text: ", with"
      - math: N = 3
      - text: using GPT-4.1 as user simulator and
      - math: N = 3
      - text: using Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct (same as
      - link "SectionЛңC.1" [ref=e199]:
      - text: ). We then only keep rollouts that successfully solved the tasks as expert rollouts. For reflection data in SR, we follow the same procedure as ALFWorld (
      - link "SectionЛңB.1" [ref=e200] [nth=3]:
      - text: ). Similar to ALFWorld, due to the weak performance of the target model on the benchmark, we use GPT-4.1 instead of Qwen3-8B to generate reflection data. All training is done on 8xB200 GPUs.
    - heading "Appendix D More Details on Ablation Studies" [ref=e201] [level=2]
    - paragraph:
      - text: For LLM-as-a-judge in
      - link "TableЛң4" [ref=e202] [nth=2]:
      - text: ", we present an example prompt and an example (hacked) response from ALFWorld in"
      - link "TableЛңA8" [ref=e203]:
      - text: . We note that the model being trained is Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, where as the judge model is Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct. In general, we find LLM-as-a-judge can be unreliable, awarding high scores to predictions that does not show genuine understanding of the environment dynamics relevant to the task.
    - heading "Appendix E More Details on Parameter Change Analysis" [ref=e204] [level=2]
    - 'figure "Figure A1: Parameter change analysis across datasets and training variants. Top: layer-wise weight change ratios by transformer layer. Bottom: main module-wise change ratios aggregated across layers."':
      - figure "(a) Layer-wise parameter change ratios by transformer layer (ALFWorld and П„ 2 -Bench).":
      - figure "(b) Module-wise parameter change ratios aggregated across layers (attention Q/K/V/O and MLP projections).":
      - text: "Figure A1: Parameter change analysis across datasets and training variants. Top: layer-wise weight change ratios by transformer layer. Bottom: main module-wise change ratios aggregated across layers."
    - paragraph:
      - text: We report a fine-grained analysis of parameter updates at both the layer and module levels. Specifically, we compute the fraction of parameters exhibiting major changes within each transformer layer, with results summarized in
      - link "FigureЛң1(a)" [ref=e205]:
      - text: . Across both benchmarks, we observe highly consistent trends throughout the network depth. Models trained with RWML display the lowest proportion of updated parameters, whereas WM SFT leads to noticeably broader parameter modifications. This pattern aligns with the forgetting behavior analyzed in
      - link "SectionЛң3.3" [ref=e206] [nth=2]:
      - text: ", where RWML models retain prior knowledge more effectively."
    - paragraph: In addition, we compare the effects of policy RL when applied to different mid-trained initializations. Notably, the parameter change profiles of policy RL remain largely similar regardless of whether RWML is applied beforehand, and closely resemble those obtained when policy RL is performed directly on the base model. In contrast, initializing policy RL from a WM SFT model results in substantially elevated update ratios. These results indicate that RWML preserves a parameter configuration that is more amenable to subsequent policy optimization, reducing the extent of disruptive updates during post-training.
    - paragraph:
      - text: To further validate that this behavior is not an artifact of layer aggregation, we conduct a module-wise breakdown over attention projections (Q/K/V/O) and MLP projection parameters, as shown in
      - link "FigureЛң1(b)" [ref=e207]:
      - text: . The observed trends closely mirror the layer-wise results, suggesting that the relative stability induced by RWML is consistent across different architectural components rather than being localized to specific modules.
    - paragraph: Overall, these detailed analyses provide additional evidence that employing RL during both mid-training and post-training leads to more coherent and stable parameter update than the conventional SFT-then-RL pipeline.
    - paragraph: Finally, we emphasize that the analyses presented here are empirical and are intended to provide descriptive evidence rather than a complete mechanistic explanation. While the observed parameter update patterns offer insights into how RWML influences subsequent training dynamics, a more systematic understanding of RL-based trainingвҖ”particularly from the perspectives of optimization dynamics and mechanistic interpretabilityвҖ”remains an important direction for future work. We hope these findings motivate further investigation into the internal representations and learning trajectories induced by RL at different stages of model training.

    - heading "Abstract" [ref=e12] [level=6]
    - paragraph:
      - text: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is a crucial technique for aligning language models with human preferences, playing a pivotal role in the success of conversational models like GPT-4, ChatGPT, and Llama 2. A core challenge in employing RLHF lies in training a reliable reward model (RM), which relies on high-quality labels typically provided by human experts or advanced AI system. These methods can be costly and may introduce biases that affect the language modelвҖҷs responses. As language models improve, human input may become less effective in further enhancing their performance. In this paper, we propose Self-Evolved Reward Learning (SER), a novel approach where the RM generates additional training data to iteratively improve itself. We conducted extensive experiments on multiple datasets such as HH-RLHF and UltraFeedback, using models like Mistral and Llama 3, and compare SER against various baselines. Our results demonstrate that even with limited human-annotated data, learning from self-feedback can robustly enhance RM performance, thereby boosting the capabilities of large language models (LLMs). Resources of this paper can be found at
      - link "https://aka.ms/ser" [ref=e13]:
    - heading "1 Introduction" [ref=e14] [level=2]
    - paragraph:
      - text: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is a well-established approach that aligns Large Language Models (LLMs) with human preference data Ouyang et al. (
      - link "2022" [ref=e15]:
      - text: ); Bai et al. (
      - link "2022b" [ref=e16]:
      - text: ). The standard approach involves learning a reward model (RM) from human preferences and the learned RM is then frozen to train LLMs via Reinforcement Learning (RL) such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) Schulman et al. (
      - link "2017a" [ref=e17]:
      - text: ). Another common approach directly trains LLMs from the human preference data without learning an RM such as Direct Preference Optimiztion (DPO) Rafailov et al. (
      - link "2024" [ref=e18]:
      - text: ). Both approaches rely heavily on the size and quality of human-annotated preference data. However, the availability of such data is often limited and expensive to acquire, posing a significant bottleneck in the development and performance of RL approaches Yuan et al. (
      - link "2024b" [ref=e19]:
      - text: ). This dependency on human-annotated data hinders the scalability of strong LLMs that require vast amounts of labeled data to achieve greater performance Kaplan et al. (
      - link "2020" [ref=e20]:
      - text: ); Muennighoff et al. (
      - link "2024" [ref=e21] [nth=1]:
      - text: ). To mitigate the dependency, recent works leverage the AI feedback to train RMs, referred to as Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback (RLAIF) Bai et al. (
      - link "2022b" [ref=e22] [nth=1]:
      - text: ); Lee et al. (
      - link "2023" [ref=e23]:
      - text: ) , which reduces the reliance on human-annotated data. However, they hold heuristic assumptions that LLMs can provide high-quality feedback and they often requires stronger LLMs to provide feedback Pang et al. (
      - link "2023" [ref=e24] [nth=1]:
      - text: ).
    - paragraph:
      - text: Recent advancements suggest that LLMs have the potential to serve as world models to a certain degree, capable of understanding world knowledge and complex patterns independently of explicit human input Hao et al. (
      - link "2023" [ref=e25] [nth=2]:
      - text: ); Guan et al. (
      - link "2023" [ref=e26] [nth=3]:
      - text: ); Zhao et al. (
      - link "2024" [ref=e27] [nth=2]:
      - text: ). Leveraging this ability, LLMs can evaluate and provide feedback. In the context of RLHF and RLAIF, this capability of LLMs can be extended as the role of RMs, and RL approaches rely heavily on the RMs Dewey (
      - link "2014" [ref=e28]:
      - text: ); Li (
      - link "2017" [ref=e29]:
      - text: ). Focusing on training a better RM with limited human-annotated data, we propose a novel reward learning approach, which self-evolves the RM through a feedback loop using the RM itself. In our approach, the LLM serves as the RM, generating feedback on the dataset that is subsequently used to refine its own learning. This iterative вҖқfeedback-then-trainвҖқ loop allows the RM to self-evolve over time, gradually improving its performance, even with some noise in the initial self-labeled data. As the iteration progresses, however, similar data offers diminishing help and can even degrade performance. To address this, we identify the RM learning status in each iteration and introduce data filtering strategies to select high-confidence data that are later used for a more robust RM training.
    - 'figure "Figure 1: The Self-Evolved Reward Learning (SER) pipeline. Our SER method consists of following steps: (1) Self-labeling: the reward model (RM) assigns labels to unlabeled data. (2) Identifying learning status and selecting data: high-confidence data is selected by assessing the learning status. (3) Retrain the RM: the RM trains itself using the self-labeled and selected data. (4) Train the Large Language Model (LLM): the LLM is trained under the guidance of the self-evolved RM. Note that steps (1)-(3) iterate multiple rounds to a converged RM."':
      - img "Refer to caption"
      - text: "Figure 1: The Self-Evolved Reward Learning (SER) pipeline. Our SER method consists of following steps: (1) Self-labeling: the reward model (RM) assigns labels to unlabeled data. (2) Identifying learning status and selecting data: high-confidence data is selected by assessing the learning status. (3) Retrain the RM: the RM trains itself using the self-labeled and selected data. (4) Train the Large Language Model (LLM): the LLM is trained under the guidance of the self-evolved RM. Note that steps (1)-(3) iterate multiple rounds to a converged RM."
    - paragraph: "By employing this self-evolved reward learning process, where the RM continually learns from its own feedback, we reduce dependency on large human-labeled data while maintaining, or even improving, the modelвҖҷs performance. Our contributions are threefold:"
    - list:
      - listitem:
      - listitem:
      - listitem:
    - paragraph: We conducted experiments on multiple datasets and LLMs with their varied sizes to validate the generalization and effectiveness of our method. We find that, compared to the seed models that use only a small amount of human-labeled data, our method can robustly and significantly enhance model performance, with an average improvement of 7.88%. After multiple iterations, the final convergence can achieve or even surpass the performance of models using the entire human-annotated dataset, providing a potential solution for the self-improvement of models.
    - heading "2 Related Work" [ref=e30] [level=2]
    - heading "2.1 Reinforcement Learning from External Feedback" [ref=e31] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: Preference learning or now commonly referred to as reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) Christiano et al. (
      - link "2017" [ref=e32] [nth=1]:
      - text: ); Ziegler et al. (
      - link "2019" [ref=e33]:
      - text: ); Stiennon et al. (
      - link "2020b" [ref=e34]:
      - text: ); Ouyang et al. (
      - link "2022" [ref=e35] [nth=1]:
      - text: ); Bai et al. (
      - link "2022a" [ref=e36]:
      - text: ) train a fixed reward model (RM) from human preference data, and the trained RM is then used to train the Large Language Model (LLM) via RL, such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) Schulman et al. (
      - link "2017b" [ref=e37]:
      - text: ). In order to make RL training more stable and efficient, methods such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) Rafailov et al. (
      - link "2024" [ref=e38] [nth=3]:
      - text: ) directly train the LLM using human preferences without training the RM. Other methods Zhao et al. (
      - link "2023" [ref=e39] [nth=4]:
      - text: ); Gulcehre et al. (
      - link "2023" [ref=e40] [nth=5]:
      - text: ); Yuan et al. (
      - link "2024a" [ref=e41]:
      - text: ) adjust the preference training schemes to improve the performance and stability. However, obtaining human preference data, especially high-quality data, is extremely expensive and time-consuming KГ¶pf et al. (
      - link "2024" [ref=e42] [nth=4]:
      - text: ); Xu et al. (
      - link "2023" [ref=e43] [nth=6]:
      - text: ); Sun et al. (
      - link "2024" [ref=e44] [nth=5]:
      - text: ), and the data diversity is skewed to be low, containing few expert-annotated data which requires huge effort and expertise Peng et al. (
      - link "2023" [ref=e45] [nth=7]:
      - text: ); Zhang et al. (
      - link "2023" [ref=e46] [nth=8]:
      - text: ); Xu et al. (
      - link "2023" [ref=e47] [nth=9]:
      - text: ). The data quality and size sets the bottleneck of the performance of LLMs. Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback (RLAIF) Bai et al. (
      - link "2022b" [ref=e48] [nth=2]:
      - text: ); Lee et al. (
      - link "2023" [ref=e49] [nth=10]:
      - text: ) employs LLMs to generate feedback for training RMs, reducing reliance on human-annotated data. However, it relies on the heuristic assumption that LLMs can provide high-quality and diverse feedback and often requires stronger LLMs to provide feedback Pang et al. (
      - link "2023" [ref=e50] [nth=11]:
      - text: ). In this paper, we leverage a small percentage of the human-annotated data to train an RM which achieves a comparable performance with the one trained with the full annotated data. The RM is further used in PPO to train the LLM.
    - heading "2.2 Self-learning in LLMs" [ref=e51] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: As the LLMs are developing towards superhuman-level, which may be bottlenecked by human performance level. Similar to the self-improvement in human reflection, self-learning is a new approch in improving LLM performance recently. Self-learning in LLMs focuses on enhancing capabilities without external supervision. SELF-ALIGN Sun et al. (
      - link "2024" [ref=e52] [nth=6]:
      - text: ) demonstrates self-alignment through principle-driven reasoning, allowing models to adjust their outputs based on internal guidelines. ReSTEM Singh et al. (
      - link "2023" [ref=e53] [nth=12]:
      - text: ) employs self-training to enhance problem-solving abilities. RLC Pang et al. (
      - link "2023" [ref=e54] [nth=13]:
      - text: ) and SCoRe Kumar et al. (
      - link "2024" [ref=e55] [nth=7]:
      - text: ) showcase methods for self-correction and improvement using self-generated data. Additionally, Huang et al. (
      - link "2022" [ref=e56] [nth=2]:
      - text: ) illustrates how LLMs can refine reasoning through self-generated rationale-augmented answers, enhancing their explanatory depth. Math-Shepherd Wang et al. (
      - link "2024" [ref=e57] [nth=8]:
      - text: ) and Self-Rewarding Language Models Yuan et al. (
      - link "2024b" [ref=e58] [nth=1]:
      - text: ) demonstrate self-rewarding mechanisms, where the model has the ability to provide high-quality rewards to itself. Our proposed approach falls in this self-learning paradigm by innovatively using the RM to generate feedback for itself, fostering robust RM training and improvement.
    - heading "3 Self-Evolved Reward Learning for Large Language Models" [ref=e59] [level=2]
    - paragraph: In this section, we present our proposed Self-Evolved Reward Learning (SER) for LLMs. This approach enables the RM to iteratively improve itself by learning from its own high-confidence predictions, thereby reducing the need for extensive human-annotated data. Initially, the RM is trained with a small set of human-annotated data to provide a basic understanding of good and bad answers. From there, the RM evolves through self-labeling and iterative retraining. The enhanced RM is then employed to guide the LLM training via RL approaches. We detail each component of our method in the below section, including self-labeling, identifying learning status, data filtering, RM retraining and the LLM training via RL with improved and converged RM.
    - heading "3.1 Overview" [ref=e60] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: Figure
      - link "1" [ref=e61]:
      - text: "illustrates the overall pipeline of our SER method. This iterative process ensures that both the reward model and the LLM are continuously refined throughout the training cycle. Our method for Reward Model training consists of the following three iterative steps:"
    - list:
      - listitem:
      - listitem:
      - listitem:
    - paragraph: With a few iterations of self-evolved reward learning, the RM training converges or meets the stopping criteria, such as when no further data can be filtered, the RM is then used to guide the training of the LLM via RL approaches. The modified PPO algorithm incorporates the evolved reward signals to optimize the LLMвҖҷs policy.
    - paragraph: "Our method relies on two distinct learning statuses for the RM: (1) the ability to distinguish between clearly good and bad answers, and (2) the ability to refine differences between answers of similar quality. These statuses are separated for the following reasons: (a) Targeted Skill Development: by recognizing different learning statuses, the RM can focus on specific skill sets. Initially, the model focuses on clear distinctions (e.g., good vs. bad answers), and as training progresses, it refines its comparative abilities with more subtle distinctions. (b) Adaptive Data Filtering: the data filtering process is driven by the current learning status, allowing the model to train on the most relevant data. This adaptive approach ensures the model always works on improving the appropriate aspect of its performance. (c) Improved Self-Evaluation: by continuously monitoring its learning status, the RM can determine when to shift from one learning focus to another. This dynamic approach fosters self-driven, curriculum-like learning."
    - paragraph: Furthermore, by allowing the RM to judge two answers for each question, the RM is provided with paired examples that are key to both learning statuses, enabling the RM to improve its discrimination and comparative abilities. Once the RM becomes proficient at handling both tasks, it is well-equipped to guide the LLM during reinforcement learning.

We use these reward scores to determine the current training status and data selection strategy. Initially, we employ a small amount of human-annotated data to obtain a seed RM. In this study, the seed RM is trained using 15% of the entire dataset.

    - heading "3.2 Theoretical Analysis" [ref=e66] [level=3]
    - paragraph:
      - text: In this section, we analyze the theoretical feasibility of SER, focusing on the convergence properties of both RM training and PPO training. A detailed theoretical analysis supporting the effectiveness of our SER method is presented in Appendix
      - link "A" [ref=e67]:
      - text: ". The key conclusions of this analysis are as follows: (a) Convergence of the Reward Model: we demonstrate that, under reasonable assumptions, the RM iteratively improves by selecting high-confidence data based on predicted probabilities. This process ensures that its performance either improves or remains stable over time. (b) Convergence of PPO with a Learned Reward Model: we establish that PPO converges to a near-optimal policy even when the RM is trained through self-labeling, provided that reward estimation errors are small. These findings confirm that both the reward model and the LLM are capable of achieving high performance with minimal human supervision."
    - heading "4 Experiment" [ref=e68] [level=2]
    - paragraph:
      - text: In this section, we report our main experiment results, including reward modeling results and PPO results. We select multiple base models with different parameter sizes (Llama 3 8B (Dubey et al.,

      - link "2023" [ref=e70] [nth=14]:
